coredata.dc:title: A high performance N-doped carbon quantum dots/5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) fluorescent sensor for biothiols detection
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Junsheng
Wu, Huixiang
Yang, Ping
Hou, Changjun
Huo, Danqun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple, fast and sensitive fluorescent sensor based on N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) was fabricated for selective detection of biothiols. NCQDs were successfully prepared via hydrothermal method and characterized using TEM, XRD, Raman spectrometer, FT-IR and XPS. In the presence of biothiols in the mixture of NCQDs and DTNB, −SH of biothiols can interact with DTNB and subsequently affording yellow-colored 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoate (TNB), which can strongly absorb excitation light of NCQDs and lead to fluorescence quenching of NCQDs. As a consequence, quantitative screening of biothiols can be achieved. Under optimized conditions, good linear relationships in the range of 0.05–40μM was obtained for L-cysteine (L-Cys), 0.01–40μM for glutathione (GSH), and 0.05–40μM for homocysteine (HCys). The detection limits of this assay for L-Cys, GSH and HCys were 1.01nM, 3.23nM, and 3.29nM, respectively. And selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the sensor were also evaluated. These results demonstrated that the presented fluorescent sensor can be a promising sensing platform for biothiols analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NCQDs
DTNB
Biothiols
Fluorescence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Chongqing science and technology commission ( CSTC2015shmszxl20097 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 81271930 and 81171414 ), Key Technologies R&D Program of China (No. 2012BAI19B03 ), Chongqing Graduate Student Research Innovation Project (No. CYB16038 ), the workstation in Sichuan Province GY2015-01 and sharing fund of Chongqing university's large equipment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fluorescence detection of biothiols based on NCQDs.
HRTEM image (A) and XRD (B) of the as-obtained NCQDs.
FT-IR spectrum of the as-obtained NCQDs.
XPS spectra of NSCDs. full scan XPS spectrum (A). High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (B), N 1s (C) and O 1s(D).

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of NCQDs, absorption spectrum of TNB; (B) Fluorescence spectra of NCQDs and NCQDs incubated with L-Cys, DTNB, and mixture of L-Cys and DTNB. Inset: photo image of mixture of NCQDs and DTNB before and after incubated with L-Cys.
Chemical structure and FT-IR spectrum of TNB.
Fluorescence lifetime of NCQDs, NCQDs/DTNB, NCQDs/DTNB/L-Cys.
Plots of fluorescence changes versus reaction time (A) and pH of solutions (B) for L-Cys.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCQDs in the presence of different concentration of L-Cys (from 0 to 120μM); (B) △F of mixture of NSCDs and DTNB incubated with different concentration of L-Cys versus the concentration of L-Cys. Inset: the linear region of the curve.

(A) UV-vis spectra of NCQDs/DTNB in the presence of different concentration of L-Cys (from 0 to 250μM); (B) absorbance at 404nm of NSCDs/DTNB incubated with different concentrations of L-Cys versus the concentration of L-Cys. Inset: the linear region of the curve.
Corresponding fluorescence change of the mixture of NCQDs and DTNB for various analytes.
Biothiols sensing using different sensor systems.
Application of the proposed fluorescent for the determination of biothiols in human serum samples spiked with different amounts of biothiols.

head.ce:title: A high performance N-doped carbon quantum dots/5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) fluorescent sensor for biothiols detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biorheology Science and Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Bioengineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biorheology Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center of Solid-State Brewing, Luzhou Laojiao Group Co. Ltd., Luzhou, Sichuan, 646000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Solid-State Brewing

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors made equal contribution to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a simple, fast and sensitive fluorescent sensor based on N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) was fabricated for selective detection of biothiols. NCQDs were successfully prepared via hydrothermal method and characterized using TEM, XRD, Raman spectrometer, FT-IR and XPS. In the presence of biothiols in the mixture of NCQDs and DTNB, −SH of biothiols can interact with DTNB and subsequently affording yellow-colored 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoate (TNB), which can strongly absorb excitation light of NCQDs and lead to fluorescence quenching of NCQDs. As a consequence, quantitative screening of biothiols can be achieved. Under optimized conditions, good linear relationships in the range of 0.05–40μM was obtained for L-cysteine (L-Cys), 0.01–40μM for glutathione (GSH), and 0.05–40μM for homocysteine (HCys). The detection limits of this assay for L-Cys, GSH and HCys were 1.01nM, 3.23nM, and 3.29nM, respectively. And selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the sensor were also evaluated. These results demonstrated that the presented fluorescent sensor can be a promising sensing platform for biothiols analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Biothiols, for example, L-cysteine (L-Cys), glutathione (GSH) and homocysteine (HCys), play significant roles in a number of biological and physiological processes in living creatures [1–3]. However, abnormal level of biothiols can lead to a series of diseases [1]. For instance, deficiency of L-Cys is involved in skin lesions, lethargy, and fat loss etc. [4]; concentrations of HCys are deemed to relate to cardiovascular and Alzheimer's disease [5]; some diseases, including cancer, aging, heart problems and some other ailments maybe resulted from the abnormal levels of GSH [6]. Therefore, detection of biothiols levels is of great importance for human health.

To date, numerous sensor systems for biological thiols discrimination have been successfully developed. Biothiols identification based on large-scale equipment, including hydrophilic interaction chromatography coupled with electrospray tandem mass spectrometry (HILIC–MS/MS) [7], electrospray ionization tandem mass spectrometry (ESI–MS/MS) [8,9], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [1,10,11] were widely studied. In spite of precision, sensitivity and broad linearity range, they were suffered from several drawbacks, including time consumption, complex sample pretreatment and manpower consumption, which greatly hinder their point-of-care and in-field applications. Therefore, development of a simple, rapid and cost-effective sensor system for the detection of biothiols is in urgent necessity.

Currently, electrochemical sensor systems also have drawn increasing attention for the detection of biothiols, owing to their low cost, high sensitivity and portability [3,4,12–14]. Nevertheless, robustness and selectivity are still to be bottlenecks. Ellman’s reagent, 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB), have been proved to be an effective chemical in employment of biothiols identification because of its sensitive and selective response towards −SH [15,16]. Mustafa et al. [15] presented a colorimetric sensor based on DTNB functionalized gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the quantitative analysis of biothiols. Briefly, a light red DTNB-AuNPs solution changed to a yellow one after addition of biothiols, which is attributed to a resultant, yellow-colored 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoate (TNB), induced by a reaction between biothiols and DTNB. Although selectivity of the sensor can be obtained base on DTNB-AuNPs, there is also some other problems. False positive signals may occur due to tendency of accumulation of AuNPs in real samples and relatively low sensitivity would hinder its generalization in real applications.

Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) have drawn more and more attentions since their first successful preparation and were utilized for catalysis, imaging and sensing because of their excellent surface properties and fluorescence [17–20]. Recently, a fluorescent sensor for L-Cys detection based on NCQDs and AuNPs with low detection limit of 20nM using mechanism of nanometal surface energy transfer (NSET) was presented [21]. Also, a ligand exchange based switch-on CNQDs-Hg2+ system employed for effective GSH identification with detection limit of 37nM was reported [22]. Among them, favourable sensitivities of these NCQDs-based sensors were acquired, robustness of the sensor may still be a problem needed to be solved.

Combining advantages of selectivity of DTNB and sensitivity of NCQDs, herein, we fabricated a simple and effective fluorescent sensor system based on NCQDs and DTNB for biothiols detection. As shown in schematic 1, NCQDs with optimal excitation wavelength and fluorescence emission wavelength of 409nm and 512nm respectively were prepared and served as signal generator. Hybrid of NCQDs and DTNB displays strong green-fluorescence with excitation of UV lamp. Upon addition of biothiols in the mixture, TNB was generated due to interaction between DTNB and −SH, which lead to a fluorescence quench owing to a strong absorbance of TNB toward exciting light accompanied with a color changed from colorless to yellow. Thus quantitative analysis for biothiols was achieved according to intensity changes of fluorescence. The results obtained demonstrated that the sensor system as-presented displayed high sensitivity and capability of disturbance and would be a promising candidate for the biothiols analysis in real samples.

Ethylenediamine was supplied by Chongqing Chuandong Chemical(Group)Co., LTD. DTNB was bought from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. L-Csy was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. GSH and HCys were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (USA) and TCI (Shanghai) Industrial Development Co., Ltd., respectively. Serine, threonine, phenylalanine, aspartic acid, valine, isoleucine, arginine, histidine, alanine, glucose, galactose, KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, MgSO4, CuCl2, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and Rhodamine B were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. Tirs-HCl and human serum were obtained from Solarbio Tech. Co. Ltd. Dialysis bag (MD31, 500D) was supplied by Shanghai Titans Polytron Technologies Inc. All above mentioned reagents were in analytical pure unless otherwise stated. All aqueous solutions were freshly prepared with deionized (DI) water (18MΩcm) from Millipore water system.

Synthesis of NCQDs was performed according to the previous report [23]. Briefly, catechol (0.27g) and ethanediamine (300μL) were dissolved in ultrapure water (25mL) with ultrasonic treatment for 5min. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred and sealed into a 200mL teflonlined autoclave and incubated at 180°C for 12h. A yellow solution was obtained after its cool to room temperature. Superfluous chemicals were removed via centrifugation with 12000rpm for 10min. Finally, NCQDs solution was acquired after a 48-h dialysis and stored at 4°C refrigerator for further utilization.

The surface morphology and crystal structures of NCQDs were characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (ZEISS LIBRA 200) and X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Bruker AXS D5005), respectively. The Nicolet 550 FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu Scientific Instruments) was performed for identification of surface function group of as-prepared NCQDs. Thermo Fischer DXR was employed for recording the Raman spectrum. And X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses (GG314-JPS-9200) were conducted for the confirmation of chemical composition of the NCQDs.

The proposed fluorescent sensor for biothiols detection was performed as following. In detail, 300μL analytes Tirs-HCl buffer solution (0.01M) with different concentrations or blank were mixed into the reaction system, which consists of 2.6mL of NCQDs (dissolved by Tirs-HCl buffer) and 100μL of DTNB (1mM). And then, the reaction solution was shaken and incubated for a certain time at room temperature. Finally, the fluorescence of samples obtained were recorded using fluorospectro photometer with excitation wavelength of 409nm, and the excitation and emission slit widths of 5 and 7nm, respectively.

Serum samples (healthy people, Solarbio Tech. Co. Ltd.) were collected and stored frozen before use. Before analysis, serum was diluted 30 times (1:30 diluted with 0.01M Tirs-HCl buffer solution, pH 7.0). And as-obtained serum were spiked with different concentrations (4, 20, 40μM) biothiols to acquire real samples. Real samples detection were carried out described as part 2.4 IFE-based fluorescent detection of biothiols.

The NCQDs were easily synthesized with catechol and ethanediamine served as precursor. As shown in Fig. 2
, TEM image and XRD of NCQDs identify their morphologies and microstructures. Fig. 2A indicated that NCQDs display relatively uniform in the shape with average grain diameter of about 3.1nm (shown in Fig. 2B). We can see from Fig. 2C, XRD pattern displays a diffraction peak at 2θ=25.3°, which corresponds to the (002) crystal planes of graphene, with lattice spacing of about 0.37nm. And Fig. 2D showed the Raman spectrum of NCQDs. Two typical Raman scattering peaks at 1536.27cm−1 and 1375.68cm−1 were ascribed to sp3 defect and sp2 hybridization of carbon, respectively [24,25]. FT-IR was also carried out to verify the surface function groups of NCQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 , a strong and broad absorption peak appears at 3390.39cm−1, which wsa originated from stretching vibrations of OH, NH. It demonstrated that NCQDs are rich in hydroxyl and amidogen on the surface of NCQDs, giving them good hydrophilic properties. And two strong absorption peaks at 2924.64cm−1 and 2854.25cm−1 were observed, which can be assigned to stretching vibration of CH3– and CH2–. Peaks appear at 1608.89cm−1 and 1520.66cm−1 were assigned to vibration of carbon skeleton of benzene ring in catechol. And two characteristic stretch band of CN bond and CN appeared at 1457.50cm−1 and 1344.68cm−1, respectively. The peaks at 1231.86cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO groups. To identify the chemical structure of NCQDs, XPS was further performed. Fig. 4 A displays the full scan XPS spectrum of NCQDs. Three sharp peaks at 285.08eV, 399.58eV and 532.68eV, correspond to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. Those observations indicated that N was successfully doped into the CQDs yielding NCQDs. The high resolution scans of the C 1s region (shown in Fig. 4B) with four different peaks imply that carbon is in three different chemical states. The peak near 284.78eV suggests the presence of graphitic structure (sp2 CC) of the NCQDs. The peak at around 285.98eV can be attributed to sp3-bonded carbon with nitrogen (CN), while that one centered at 287.48eV can be assigned to CO. And peak at 288.78eV was ascribed to CO. As displayed in Fig. 4C, the N 1s spectrum shows three peaks, centered at 398.78eV, 399.78eV and 401.08eV, were respectively linked to the NH, CN and CN bands. High resolution of the O 1s spectrum (as shown in Fig. 4D) of NSCDs clearly exhibits three peaks, in which centered at 530.98eV and 532.18eV and 533.28eV, and can be assigned to OH, CO, and CO, respectively. All these results demonstrated the NCQDs in good monodispersity with high crystallinity were successfully prepared.

NCQDs fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was obtained by comparing the wavelength integrated intensity of testing sample to that of an aqueous solution of Rhodamine B (QY of 89% at 410nm, n=1.33). The QY is calculated using following equation:
Q Y s a m = Q Y r e f
I s a m
I r e f
A ref
A sam
n
s a m
2
n
r e f
2
Wherein, I refers to the integrated emission intensity, A represents absorbance measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer, and n is refractive index of the solvent, “sam” and “ref” represent sample and reference. According to above equation, QY of the NCQDs was determined to be 29% (Table S1).

Mechanism of the presented sensor were shown in Fig. 1 and Fig. 5 . Fig. 5A shows excitation peak of 409nm and fluorescent emission peak of 512nm for NCQDs, and a strong and broad absorption peak of 407nm for TNB. A well overlap between excitation peak of NCQDs and absorption of TNB was observed. As depicted in Fig. 5B, NCQDs show strong and green fluorescence in absence of L-Cys, suggesting scare influence of L-Cys on fluorescence of NCQDs. Similarly, a slight fluorescence quench for NCQDs can be observed with presence of DTNB, which maybe resulted from a weak decomposition of DTNB into TNB. Upon addition of L-Cys into the mixture of NCQDs and DTNB, reaction between −SH of L-Cys and DTNB occurred subsequently yielding a yellow colored TNB. TNB display a strong absorption at 407nm and can absorb excitation light at 409nm, leading to a significant fluorescence quenching. Chemical structure and FT-IR spectrum of TNB was shown in Fig. 6 . Peaks at 1521.58cm−1 and 1342.24cm−1 were assigned to asymmetric stretching vibration and symmetric stretching vibration of −NO2, respectively. A strong peak at 1613.19cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO. And peak appear at 3430.79cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH. Particularly, S-H stretching vibration appeared at 2425.09cm−1. This FT-IR confirm the chemical structure of TNB. Therefore qualitative and quantitative analysis for L-Cys can be achieved based on the fluorescence changes. Photo image of NCQDs under UV lamp in inset of Fig. 5B intuitively confirm significant fluorescence quenching NCQDs after their interaction with L-Cys. Similar observations were also obtained for GSH (Fig.S1) and HCys (Fig.S2). Additionally, mechanism of fluorescent sensor also can be proved by fluorescence lifetime. As shown in Fig. 7 , fluorescence lifetime of NCQDs was 3.82ns. No obvious lifetime decay for after incubation with DTNB (3.55ns) and DTNB/L-Cys (3.59ns), indicating that static quenching of the NCQDs in turn-off process [26]. These demonstrated that the sensor system as-fabricated would be effective for biothiols detection.

In order to optimize the as-fabricated fluorescent sensor for the quantitative detection of biothiols, effect of incubation time and pH of tris-HCl buffer solution were systematically investigated, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8 A, the effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity changes of sensor system at 512nm varying from 0 to 5min with presence of L-Cys were examined. Initially, no obvious fluorescence change was observed at the moment of addition of L-Cys. After a one-minute incubation, an intense fluorescence change was obtained. And after 2min of reaction time, the signal levelled off, indicating that the reaction reaches saturation. Given that the demand of rapid detection, 2min of reaction time was selected as optimal one for further studies. Fig. 8 B depicted the effect of pH of tris-HCl buffer solution on analytical performance. In the range of pH of 3–7, intensities of fluorescence changes gradually increased with increasing pH. However, fluorescence changes decreased gradually as increasing pH ranged from 7 to 11. Taking actual detection environment and the experiment results into account, pH of 7 was selected as the optimal detection condition. Furthermore, consistent optimal incubation time and pH of solutions were also acquired for GSH (Fig.S3) and HCys (Fig.S4) discrimination, respectively. Generally, incubation time of 2min pH of 7 were selected as optimal detection conditions for biothiols analysis.

After the optimization of detection conditions, analytical performances of the sensor as-presented for biothiols detection were investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 9 A, well-defined fluorescent emission peaks were obtained after incubation with L-Cys in different concentrations, and the fluorescence intensities decreased gradually with the increasing concentrations ranged from 0 to 120μM. Fig. 9B shows a fine linear relationship between fluorescence changes and L-Cys concentrations ranged from 1nM to 40μM, with a low detection limit of 1.01nM. The linear equation was y=9.28x+15.24 (R2 =0.9925). Wherein, y means ΔF (fluorescence intensity change, F(NCQDs/DTNB)-F(NCQDs/DTNB/analyte)) and x represents concentrations (μM) of L-Cys. Besides, linear relations ranged from 10nM to 40μM and from 5nM to 40μM, with detection limits of 3.23nM and 3.29nM for GSH and HCys, respectively. Furthermore, L-Cys was selected as representative one to further verify sensor system. As shown in Fig. 10 A, UV–vis spectra changes of NCQDs/DTNB after addition of L-Cys with different concentrations ranging from 0μM to 250μM. It is clear that the absorption bands at 323nm decreased as well as new absorption peaks at 404nm increased gradually as increase of L-Cys concentrations. And an excellent linear relationship (R2 =0.99) for L-Cys concentrations ranged from 1 to 140μM was obtained with detection limit of 0.52μM (shown in 10B). That also verify the detection mechanism of fluorescent sensor. Notably, as shown in Table 1 , the sensor as-presented displayed relatively higher sensitivities than other sensors. The analytical performance exhibited here can be assigned from highly reactive interaction between biothiols and DTNB. In addition, easiness of operations for detection process also boost the effectiveness of the sensor on biothiols detection. These all suggested that as-fabricated sensor is reliable for ultrasensitive, fast and effective biothiols detection.

In order to investigate the feasibility of the sensor, interferences studies were performed. Serine, threonine, phenylalanine, aspartic acid, valine, isoleucine, arginine, histidine, alanine, SO4 2−, NO3 −，Cl−, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, glucose and galactose were selected for the interference chemicals. Fluorescence intensities of NCQDs solutions at 512nm after a co-incubation with different analytes for 5min were recorded. As shown in Fig. 11 , L-Cys, GSH and HCys with concentration of 40μM can largely induce fluorescence quench, while no obvious change was observed for other potential interfering substances even with much larger concentration (0.4mM), owing to interactions between −SH of biothiols and DTNB. Among them, fluorescence change for L-Cys was higher than that for GSH and HCys, which could be attributed to their relatively lower activities compared with L-Cys. These results demonstrated that the sensor fabricated here exhibits excellent capability of anti-disturbance towards biothiols.

To evaluate the reproducibility and stability of as-fabricated sensor, the sensor system were fabricated and tested every 4days for 7 times under optimal detection conditions. The relative standard deviations (RSD) of assay were 3.82%, indicating acceptable reproducibility and precision of the sensor. Additionally, fluorescence intensity of NCQDs recorded after stored at 4°C refrigerator for 1 month also displayed a good reproducibility, with 98% of their initial current signal. Thus, the sensor shows excellent stability. Furthermore, to study the potential real application, human serum samples spiked with L-Cys in various concentrations were investigated. The detection results were listed in Table 2 . Excellent recoveries varying from 96.69% to 102.28% were obtained with satisfactory repeatability. These results indicated that the fluorescent sensor presented here displayed great potential real application in biothiols analysis.

In summary, a fast and sensitive fluorescent sensor based on NCQDs and DTNB for biothiols detection was effectively fabricated. NCQDs were successfully synthesized using simple one-pot methods and displayed excellent fluorescence properties. The sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor was achieved because of highly reactive and selective interaction between −SH of biothiols and DTNB. In addition, the sensor exhibits excellent reproducibility, stability and capability of anti-disturbance for biothiols detection. These results demonstrated that the presented fluorescent sensor is a promising sensing platform for biothiols analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Chongqing science and technology commission (CSTC2015shmszxl20097), National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 81271930 and 81171414), Key Technologies R&D Program of China (No. 2012BAI19B03), Chongqing Graduate Student Research Innovation Project (No. CYB16038), the workstation in Sichuan Province GY2015-01 and sharing fund of Chongqing university's large equipment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.09.143.

ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Junsheng Yang is a graduate in Bioengineering College of Chongqing University, China. He focus on preparation of quantum dots and its application in biothiols trace sensing.

Huixiang Wu is a Ph.D. candidate in Bioengineering College of Chongqing University, China. His research field is analytical chemistry and he is focus on the preparation of inorganic/organic composites as well as their application in pesticides trace sensing.

Changjun Hou completed his Ph.D. in biomedical engineering in 2004 at Chongqing University. After a period of research at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign as a visiting scholar, he returned to China and got tenure at the college of bioengineering in Chongqing University. In 2010, he became a full professor in biomedical engineering. His scientific interests rest on the design and construction of biochemical sensing systems based on porphyrins and other sensitive materials.

Danqun Huo received Ph.D. degree in biological medical engineering from Chongqing University in 2004 and had once been visited Australia, America and Italy. She is currently Distinguished Professor of Sichuan University of science and engineering and professor of biomedical science of Chongqing University. Her current research interests include the development of chemical sensors and sensor-array, electrochemistry and analytical microsystems.
